name: OSI and TCP/IP Models
description: The seven-layer OSI model and four-layer TCP/IP model, their functions, and how they map to each other
key_facts: OSI layers (bottom to top): Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application
Layer 2 (Data Link) uses MAC addresses and includes switches; Layer 3 (Network) uses IP addresses and includes routers
TCP/IP model has four layers: Network Access, Internet, Transport, Application
Encapsulation adds headers at each layer: data becomes segments, packets, frames, then bits
PDU names: Layer 4 = segments (TCP) / datagrams (UDP), Layer 3 = packets, Layer 2 = frames
common_misconceptions: The OSI model is a protocol (it is a conceptual framework, not an actual protocol stack)
Hubs operate at Layer 2 (hubs operate at Layer 1; switches operate at Layer 2)

name: IP Addressing and Subnetting
description: IPv4 and IPv6 addressing, subnetting, CIDR notation, and address types
key_facts: CIDR notation /24 = 255.255.255.0 = 256 addresses (254 usable hosts)
IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses in eight groups of four hexadecimal digits
APIPA range 169.254.0.0/16 is used when DHCP is unavailable
Subnetting divides a network into smaller segments to reduce broadcast domains
common_misconceptions: A /24 subnet provides 256 usable host addresses (the network and broadcast addresses are reserved, leaving 254)
IPv6 is just longer IPv4 (IPv6 has fundamentally different features like SLAAC and no broadcast)

name: Switching and Routing Concepts
description: How switches and routers forward traffic, VLANs, and routing protocols
key_facts: Switches use MAC address tables to forward frames to the correct port
VLANs logically segment a network at Layer 2 to isolate broadcast domains
Trunk ports carry traffic for multiple VLANs using 802.1Q tagging
Static routing uses manually configured routes; dynamic routing uses protocols like OSPF and BGP
Default gateway is the router IP that forwards traffic destined for other networks
common_misconceptions: VLANs can communicate without a router (inter-VLAN routing requires a Layer 3 device)
Managed and unmanaged switches are the same (managed switches support VLANs, port mirroring, and SNMP)

name: Wireless Standards and Configuration
description: Wi-Fi standards, wireless security protocols, and access point configuration
key_facts: WPA3 uses SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) replacing WPA2's PSK four-way handshake
802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6) introduces OFDMA and MU-MIMO for better multi-device performance
Wireless controllers manage multiple access points centrally in enterprise environments
Channel overlap on 2.4 GHz: only channels 1, 6, and 11 are non-overlapping
Site surveys determine optimal AP placement, channel assignment, and power levels
common_misconceptions: WEP is acceptable for small networks (WEP is completely broken and should never be used)
More access points always improve performance (overlapping channels cause co-channel interference)

name: DNS and DHCP Services
description: Domain Name System resolution process and Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol operation
key_facts: DNS record types: A (IPv4), AAAA (IPv6), CNAME (alias), MX (mail), PTR (reverse lookup), TXT (text data)
DNS resolution order: local cache, hosts file, DNS server, recursive query to root/TLD/authoritative servers
DHCP provides IP address, subnet mask, default gateway, and DNS server addresses automatically
DHCP process: Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledge (DORA)
DNS uses port 53; DHCP uses ports 67 (server) and 68 (client)
common_misconceptions: DNS only resolves domain names to IP addresses (it also handles reverse lookups, mail routing, and text records)
DHCP reservations and static IPs are the same (reservations are centrally managed via DHCP, static IPs are configured on each device)

name: Network Monitoring and Documentation
description: SNMP, syslog, network monitoring tools, and documentation practices
key_facts: SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol) monitors and manages network devices using OIDs and MIBs
SNMPv3 adds authentication and encryption; SNMPv1/v2c use community strings in clear text
Syslog centralizes log collection from network devices on port 514
NetFlow and sFlow analyze traffic flow patterns for capacity planning and security
Baseline documentation establishes normal network performance metrics for comparison
common_misconceptions: SNMP community strings are passwords (they are sent in plain text in v1/v2c and provide minimal security)
Network monitoring is only for large enterprises (even small networks benefit from monitoring for uptime and security)

name: High Availability and Disaster Recovery
description: Redundancy techniques, load balancing, and disaster recovery strategies for networks
key_facts: NIC teaming (bonding) combines multiple network adapters for redundancy and throughput
First Hop Redundancy Protocols: HSRP (Cisco), VRRP (standard), GLBP (Cisco)
Load balancers distribute traffic across multiple servers for performance and fault tolerance
RPO (Recovery Point Objective) defines acceptable data loss; RTO (Recovery Time Objective) defines acceptable downtime
Hot site is fully operational; warm site has equipment but needs configuration; cold site is empty space
common_misconceptions: Load balancing and failover clustering are the same (load balancing distributes traffic; clustering provides automatic failover)
A hot site eliminates all downtime risk (switching to a hot site still takes some time)

name: Network Security Concepts
description: Firewalls, ACLs, IDS/IPS, and defense-in-depth strategies for network security
key_facts: Firewalls filter traffic based on rules using source/destination IP, port, and protocol
IDS (Intrusion Detection System) monitors and alerts; IPS (Intrusion Prevention System) monitors and blocks
ACLs (Access Control Lists) on routers filter traffic at Layer 3/4
Defense in depth uses multiple security layers: physical, network, host, application, data
DMZ (Demilitarized Zone) isolates public-facing servers between external and internal firewalls
common_misconceptions: A firewall alone provides complete network security (defense in depth requires multiple security layers)
IDS and IPS are the same (IDS only detects and alerts; IPS actively blocks threats)

name: VPN and Remote Access Technologies
description: VPN types, tunneling protocols, and secure remote access methods
key_facts: Site-to-site VPN connects entire networks; client-to-site (remote access) VPN connects individual users
IPsec operates at Layer 3 and provides encryption and authentication using AH and ESP
SSL/TLS VPN operates at Layer 4-7 and typically uses port 443 through a web browser
Split tunneling routes only corporate traffic through VPN; full tunneling routes all traffic through VPN
RADIUS and TACACS+ are AAA (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting) protocols for remote access
common_misconceptions: All VPN traffic is encrypted (AH in IPsec provides authentication but not encryption; ESP provides both)
Split tunneling is always more secure (it can expose the corporate network if the user's local network is compromised)

name: Network Troubleshooting Methodology and Tools
description: Systematic troubleshooting approach and command-line/hardware tools for diagnosing network issues
key_facts: Troubleshooting steps: Identify, Establish theory, Test theory, Plan of action, Implement, Verify, Document
ping tests reachability using ICMP echo requests/replies
traceroute/tracert maps the path packets take through the network, hop by hop
nslookup and dig query DNS servers to verify name resolution
Cable testers verify continuity and wiring standards (T568A/T568B); TDR locates cable breaks
common_misconceptions: A successful ping means no network issues (ping only tests basic reachability; latency, jitter, and packet loss require further testing)
Traceroute shows the exact path every packet takes (routing can change dynamically, so traceroute shows a snapshot)

name: Common Network Issues and Symptoms
description: Identifying and resolving common network problems including connectivity, performance, and security issues
key_facts: Duplicate IP addresses cause intermittent connectivity for both affected devices
Broadcast storms result from switching loops; STP (Spanning Tree Protocol) prevents them
Asymmetric routing causes traffic to take different paths in each direction, breaking stateful firewalls
DNS issues manifest as inability to reach sites by name while IP-based access still works
Incorrect subnet mask causes devices on the same network to be unable to communicate
common_misconceptions: Slow network always means insufficient bandwidth (latency, packet loss, and misconfiguration are common causes)
STP causes network problems (STP prevents loops; disabling STP can cause broadcast storms)
